titleEn: Lawmakers introduce bill to boost AI in farming
titleZh: 美国两党议员提出《FARM AI法案》，推动人工智能在农业中的负责任应用
summaryZh: 美国众议员Zach Nunn和Don Davis联合提出《FARM AI法案》，旨在通过现代化美国农业部的研究和劳动力计划，加速人工智能在农业中的部署。法案将AI列为优先研究领域，为推广系统提供资源帮助农民采用AI和精准农业技术，扩大农村劳动力培训资助，并设立AI顾问协调联邦工作。该法案回应了农民面临的高成本、技术短缺等障碍，有望推动智慧畜牧和种植业的智能化转型。
translationZh:
代表Zach Nunn（爱荷华州第三选区）和Don Davis（北卡罗来纳州第一选区）提出了两党合作的《通过人工智能促进农业研究和现代化法案》（FARM AI Act），该立法旨在通过现代化美国农业部的研究和劳动力计划，加速人工智能在美国农业中的部署。

“爱荷华州的农民始终是率先应用新技术的群体，只要这些技术能帮助他们更高效地种植、节约资源并保持竞争力，”Nunn代表表示。“人工智能是下一个前沿领域。从产量绘图和精准养分施用，到疾病检测和水资源保护，AI为农民提供了更好的信息，使他们能够实时做出更明智的决策。我们的两党FARM AI法案建立在爱荷华州大学已经开展的突破性研究基础上，同时投资于将这些创新从实验室带到田间所需的劳动力。农业的未来不应由华盛顿的官僚决定——而应由养活美国的爱荷华州农民来驱动。”

爱荷华州已经在农业AI研究领域处于前沿。位于爱荷华州立大学的韧性农业AI研究所，由美国国家科学基金会和美国农业部国家食品与农业研究所支持，正在开发基于AI的作物和整个农业系统模型，以帮助育种者培育更强壮的品种，并帮助生产者用更少的资源提高产量。

这一势头持续增长。最近，美国国家科学基金会宣布爱荷华州立大学将领导一个横跨爱荷华州和内布拉斯加州的新创新网络，汇集社区学院、行业合作伙伴、企业家和投资者，以推动生物经济领域的创新。尽管研究进展迅速，但许多生产者仍然面临将这些工具投入实际应用的障碍。高昂的前期成本、有限的技术专业知识以及农村劳动力培训的短缺，减缓了AI技术在许多农场的采用。FARM AI法案通过现代化现有的美国农业部计划来应对这些挑战，帮助将创新从实验室推向农场大门。

“养活美国就是国家安全，农业的未来将取决于我们创新的能力，”Davis代表说。“我们必须加强研究，扩大劳动力培训，为生产者提供提高生产力、保持全球竞争力所需的工具，并确保美国继续在农业领域引领世界。”

具体而言，FARM AI法案将：

**将人工智能指定为美国农业部农业与食品研究计划下的优先研究领域**，并通过农业高级研究与发展局扩大AI研究。

**为美国农业部推广系统配备资源**，帮助农民负责任地采用AI技术和精准农业。

**扩大美国农业部的资助和奖学金**，以加强农村劳动力，培养下一代农业创新者。

**任命一位高级美国农业部官员担任农业AI顾问**，协调联邦AI工作，协调各机构计划，并与国家标准与技术研究院合作制定农业AI国家标准。

FARM AI法案是参议员Ted Budd（共和党-北卡罗来纳州）和Adam Schiff（民主党-加利福尼亚州）在参议院提出的两党立法的众议院配套法案。

法案文本可在此处获取。
featuredReason: 该法案直接针对智慧农业推广中的关键瓶颈（成本、技术、培训），并设立国家级协调机制，对智慧畜牧和种植业的AI落地具有里程碑式的政策指导意义，值得行业从业者密切关注。
contentFull:

News

Lawmakers introduce bill to boost AI in farming

FARM AI Act would equip USDA Extension with the resources to help farmers responsibly adopt AI technologies and precision agriculture.

Industry ReleaseJuly 16, 2026

schedule3 Min Read

Getty Images

Representatives Zach Nunn (IA-03) and Don Davis (NC-01) have introduced the bipartisan Fostering Agricultural Research and Modernization through Artificial Intelligence (FARM AI) Act, legislation to accelerate the deployment of artificial intelligence (AI) across American agriculture by modernizing USDA research and workforce programs.

“Iowa farmers have always been the first to put new technology to work when it helps them grow more efficiently, conserve resources, and stay competitive,” said Rep. Nunn. “Artificial intelligence is the next frontier. From yield mapping and precision nutrient application to disease detection and water conservation, AI gives farmers better information to make better decisions in real time. Our bipartisan FARM AI Act builds on the groundbreaking research already happening at Iowa’s universities while investing in the workforce needed to bring these innovations from the lab to the field. The future of agriculture shouldn’t be written by bureaucrats in Washington — it should be driven by the Iowa farmers who feed America.”

Related:Bills reintroduced to support small-scale farmers, ag workers

Iowa is already at the forefront of AI research in agriculture. The AI Institute for Resilient Agriculture, based at Iowa State University and supported by the National Science Foundation and USDA’s National Institute of Food and Agriculture, is developing AI-powered models of crops and entire farming systems to help breeders develop stronger varieties and help producers improve yields while using fewer resources.

That momentum continues to grow. Recently, the NSF announced Iowa State University will lead a new innovation network spanning Iowa and Nebraska that brings together community colleges, industry partners, entrepreneurs and investors to spur innovation in the bioeconomy. While research is advancing rapidly, many producers still face barriers to putting these tools to work. High upfront costs, limited technical expertise and a shortage of rural workforce training have slowed adoption of AI technologies on many farms. The FARM AI Act addresses those challenges by modernizing existing USDA programs to help move innovation from the laboratory to the farmgate.

“Feeding America is national security, and the future of agriculture will depend on our ability to innovate,” said Rep. Davis. “We must strengthen research, expand workforce training and give producers the tools they need to increase productivity, remain globally competitive and ensure the United States continues to lead the world in agriculture.”

Specifically, the FARM AI Act would:

Related:Pork producers weigh in on FDA review of food additive

Designate artificial intelligence as a priority research area under USDA’s Agriculture and Food Research Initiative and expand AI research through the Agriculture Advanced Research and Development Authority.

Equip USDA Extension with the resources to help farmers responsibly adopt AI technologies and precision agriculture.

Expand USDA grants and fellowships to strengthen the rural workforce and prepare the next generation of agricultural innovators.

Nominate a senior USDA official as the AI in Agriculture Advisor to coordinate federal AI efforts, align agency programs and work with the National Institute of Standards and Technology to develop national standards for agricultural AI.

The FARM AI Act is the House companion to bipartisan legislation introduced in the Senate by Senators Ted Budd (R-NC) and Adam Schiff (D-CA).

Text of the bill is available here.
